credit_headline.narrative: Indian bank credit is firing on all cylinders — 15.89% growth in April 2026 marks near-peak expansion levels, up 1.44 points in just two months. All four major sectors are growing simultaneously, creating a broad-based lending boom rather than narrow concentration. For lenders and investors, this signals a robust credit cycle with momentum building across the entire economy.

sibc-bank-credit-abs.observation: Total bank credit reached ₹213.6 lakh crore in April 2026, the highest level on record.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.direction: Volume expanded ₹6.1 lakh crore from March → April, extending the upward trajectory from ₹178.7 lakh crore in February 2025.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.inference: Banks are deploying capital at record pace, signaling strong loan demand and institutional confidence in credit expansion.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.observation: Bank credit growth accelerated 5.1 percentage points from FY25 to FY26, the maximum acceleration in this dataset.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.direction: Acceleration has held steady at 5.1pp across all six reporting periods from February 2025 through April 2026.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.inference: This sustained acceleration indicates structural momentum rather than cyclical uptick — banks have shifted into a higher growth regime.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.observation: Banks added ₹29.6 lakh crore in absolute credit during FY26, matching the peak annual expansion.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.direction: The FY26 addition of ₹29.6 lakh crore has remained constant across all reporting periods since February 2025.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.inference: This represents the largest annual credit creation in the dataset, demonstrating banks' capacity for sustained large-scale lending.

sibc-bank-credit-yoy.observation: Bank credit grew 16.1% year-on-year in April 2026, the fastest growth rate in the four-period window.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 14.6% → 16.1% between March and April, building on the 11.0% → 14.6% climb since April 2025.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.inference: Credit is expanding at the upper end of its historical range, suggesting robust demand across borrower segments without signs of cooling.

sibc-food-credit-abs.observation: Food credit outstanding fell to ₹70,271 crore in April 2026, down ₹12,372 crore from the prior month.
sibc-food-credit-abs.direction: Volume declined from ₹82,643 crore → ₹70,271 crore, following the seasonal peak of ₹89,295 crore in February 2026.
sibc-food-credit-abs.inference: Food credit shows typical seasonal volatility tied to agricultural cycles, with April representing the post-harvest reduction in working capital needs.

sibc-food-credit-yoy.observation: Food credit grew 139.4% year-on-year in April 2026, the highest growth rate in the dataset.
sibc-food-credit-yoy.direction: Growth surged from 58.9% → 139.4% between March and April, following a steady climb from 23.1% in February 2025.
sibc-food-credit-yoy.inference: Despite low absolute volumes, food credit is expanding at explosive rates, likely reflecting policy support for agricultural lending and seasonal working capital demand.

sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.observation: Non-food credit reached ₹212.9 lakh crore in April 2026, setting a new record high.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.direction: Volume expanded ₹6.2 lakh crore from ₹206.7 lakh crore → ₹212.9 lakh crore, up from ₹178.1 lakh crore in February 2025.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.inference: Non-food credit drives virtually all system growth, indicating broad-based demand across industrial, services, and retail segments.

sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.observation: Non-food credit added ₹29.2 lakh crore during FY26, the maximum annual expansion in this dataset.
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.direction: The FY26 addition has remained constant at ₹29.2 lakh crore across all reporting periods since February 2025.
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.inference: This represents the core engine of credit growth, with banks channeling the vast majority of new lending into commercial and retail sectors rather than food-related financing.

sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.observation: Non-food credit grew 15.89% year-on-year in April 2026.
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 14.45% → 15.89%, gaining 1.44 points over two months.
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.inference: Credit expansion is at or near its highest level in this window, signaling strong lending momentum across Indian banks.

sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.observation: All 4 main sectors recorded positive year-on-year growth in April 2026.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.direction: Breadth remained steady at 4 sectors, unchanged from the prior period.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.inference: Growth is broad-based across agriculture, industry, services, and retail — no sector is dragging down the overall expansion.

sector_mix.narrative: Services credit is reshaping bank lending with 18.99% growth driving its share to 28.47%—the highest on record. NBFCs lead the expansion at 34.1% of services credit, while shipping and technology subsectors post 40%+ growth rates. Personal loans also strengthened to 16.24% growth, confirming broad-based credit momentum beyond just business lending. The economy is transitioning toward services-led growth with banks following the capital allocation.

sibc-agriculture-share.observation: Agriculture holds 12.43% of total bank credit, up 0.12 percentage points from the prior period.
sibc-agriculture-share.direction: Share ticked up from 12.30% → 12.43%, staying near its historical median level.
sibc-agriculture-share.inference: Agriculture maintains its traditional ~12.4% allocation despite strong growth momentum — banks are expanding ag lending in line with overall portfolio expansion.

sibc-agriculture-yoy.observation: Agriculture credit grew 15.70% year-on-year, the strongest pace in this 6-period dataset.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 12.33% → 15.70%, marking a +3.37 percentage point jump in one period.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.inference: This is agriculture's highest growth rate on record in our window — likely driven by strong crop cycles and renewed policy focus on rural lending.

sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.observation: Industry credit shows full-year acceleration of 6.7 percentage points, holding at its maximum level.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.direction: FY acceleration has remained steady at 6.7pp across all 6 periods in this dataset.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.inference: Industry lending has sustained its strongest structural momentum throughout this window — this isn't a cyclical spike but an entrenched uptrend.

sibc-industry-share.observation: Industry accounts for 21.52% of total credit, essentially unchanged from the prior period.
sibc-industry-share.direction: Share edged up marginally from 21.51% → 21.52%, staying near historical median levels.
sibc-industry-share.inference: Industry maintains stable portfolio allocation despite strong growth — banks are scaling industrial lending proportionally with total book expansion.

sibc-industry-yoy.observation: Industry credit grew 14.96% year-on-year, the highest growth rate in this 6-period window.
sibc-industry-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 13.54% → 14.96%, building on an already strong upward trajectory.
sibc-industry-yoy.inference: Industry credit is hitting peak growth momentum — likely reflecting infrastructure investment, manufacturing recovery, and corporate capex revival.

sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.observation: Housing Finance Companies command 18.1% of NBFC credit while Public Financial Institutions hold 16.9%.
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.direction: PFIs shifted status from active to strengthening, with a 1.2 percentage point gap between the two segments.
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.inference: The NBFC sector shows balanced allocation between housing and public finance, with PFIs gaining momentum — watch for PFIs to challenge HFC dominance.

sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Public Financial Institutions are growing at 52.5% while Housing Finance Companies expand at 15.5%.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.direction: A massive 37 percentage point growth spread separates the fastest and slowest NBFC segments.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.inference: This is an extreme divergence — PFIs are in hypergrowth mode while HFCs face structural headwinds or regulatory constraints.

sibc-personal-loans-share.observation: Personal loans represent 32.60% of total credit, down 0.28 percentage points from the prior period.
sibc-personal-loans-share.direction: Share declined from 32.88% → 32.60%, remaining the largest sector but losing ground marginally.
sibc-personal-loans-share.inference: Personal loans still dominate but are ceding share to productive sectors — a healthy rebalancing toward agriculture and industry after years of retail-led growth.

sibc-personal-loans-yoy.observation: Personal loans credit grew 16.24% year-on-year in April 2026, the strongest reading in the 6-period window.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 15.22% to 16.24%, extending the upward trajectory from 11.7% in April 2025.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.inference: Consumer lending momentum is building after a slow start to FY26, suggesting household credit demand has turned durably positive.

sibc-services-fy-acceleration.observation: Services credit maintained 7.0 percentage point full-year acceleration in April 2026, the maximum level across all periods.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.direction: FY acceleration held steady at 7.0pp for six consecutive periods since February 2025.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.inference: Services credit growth has structurally shifted higher and sustained the pace, indicating broad-based economic activity beyond manufacturing.

sibc-services-share.observation: Services captured 28.47% of total bank credit in April 2026, the highest share in the dataset.
sibc-services-share.direction: Share rose from 28.10% to 28.47%, continuing the climb from 27.7% in April 2025.
sibc-services-share.inference: Banks are allocating more capital to services as the sector outgrows traditional industry lending, reshaping credit deployment toward consumption and business services.

sibc-services-share-scan.observation: NBFCs command 34.1% of services credit and moved to strengthening status, while Trade holds 22.7% and Other Services 21.0%.
sibc-services-share-scan.direction: NBFCs advanced from active to strengthening status, widening their lead over other services subsectors.
sibc-services-share-scan.inference: The NBFC boom is driving services credit expansion, as banks fund the non-bank lenders who reach segments traditional banks cannot serve efficiently.

sibc-services-yoy.observation: Services credit grew 18.99% year-on-year in April 2026, the peak growth rate in the 6-period window.
sibc-services-yoy.direction: Growth jumped from 16.26% to 18.99%, accelerating from the 12.0% pace in April 2025.
sibc-services-yoy.inference: Services is the economy's growth engine, with credit expansion nearly double the historical norm signaling strong business investment and working capital demand.

sibc-services-yoy-scan.observation: Shipping leads services growth at 42.2% and advanced to strengthening status, followed by Computer Software at 39.0% and NBFCs at 26.3%.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.direction: Shipping moved from active to strengthening, while Computer Software, Trade, Aviation, and Other Services all shifted to strengthening status.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.inference: Growth is broadening across services with infrastructure, technology, and trade finance all accelerating simultaneously—indicating robust economic expansion rather than sector-specific bubbles.

sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.observation: Wholesale Trade holds 56.4% of trade credit and strengthened, while Retail Trade's 43.6% share weakened.
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.direction: The wholesale-retail split shifted toward wholesale, with wholesale strengthening and retail weakening in relative positioning.
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.inference: B2B commerce is capturing more credit allocation than consumer retail, suggesting supply chain financing and inventory investment are driving trade credit more than end-consumer spending.

sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Wholesale Trade grew 20.1% and Retail Trade 11.5%, both advancing to strengthening status despite the 8.6 percentage point growth gap.
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Both wholesale and retail shifted from active to strengthening, though wholesale growth is nearly double retail's pace.
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Trade finance is accelerating across both channels, but the wholesale advantage suggests businesses are building inventory and expanding operations faster than consumers are spending.

industry.narrative: Indian banks are executing a massive tilt toward small business lending — Micro & Small enterprises grew 33% year-on-year while claiming 23% of all industry credit, the highest share in this dataset. This isn't cyclical but structural: the 24.2pp acceleration versus large corporates represents the widest growth gap on record. Even traditional sectors like textiles are seeing broad-based credit expansion across all sub-segments, suggesting the small business boom extends beyond high-growth sectors into core manufacturing.

sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.observation: Iron & Steel commands 68.8% of basic metal credit while Other Metal Products holds 31.2%.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.direction: Iron & Steel strengthened its position while Other Metal Products weakened, creating a 37.7 percentage point spread between them.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.inference: Traditional steel is consolidating its credit dominance within metals, likely reflecting infrastructure and construction demand over specialty metal applications.

sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Other Metal Products grew 21.0% year-on-year versus Iron & Steel at 18.7%.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Growth rates are tightly clustered with just a 2.2 percentage point spread between the segments.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Despite Iron & Steel's share dominance, specialty metal products are expanding slightly faster, suggesting emerging demand beyond core infrastructure steel.

sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.observation: Others leads chemical credit at 44.2%, followed by Drugs & Pharmaceuticals at 33.9%, with Petro Chemicals at 11.9% and Fertiliser at 9.9%.
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.direction: Drugs & Pharmaceuticals strengthened while Fertiliser weakened, with a 34.3 percentage point spread across the four segments.
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.inference: Pharma is gaining credit allocation within chemicals while fertiliser loses ground, reflecting the shift toward higher-value chemical manufacturing over agricultural inputs.

sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Petro Chemicals leads chemical growth at 27.7%, Drugs & Pharmaceuticals at 17.9%, while Fertiliser contracts -4.5%.
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.direction: A 32.2 percentage point growth spread separates the fastest and slowest segments, with Fertiliser in outright decline.
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Credit is flowing toward downstream petrochemicals and pharma manufacturing while agricultural fertiliser faces headwinds, marking a clear value-chain rotation within chemicals.

sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.observation: Other Engineering dominates at 81.1% of engineering credit while Electronics holds just 18.9%.
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.direction: Other Engineering strengthened its position while Electronics weakened, creating a 62.2 percentage point gap.
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.inference: Traditional engineering sectors are capturing credit over electronics manufacturing, suggesting infrastructure and machinery demand outweighs tech hardware investment.

sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Other Engineering grows 37.5% year-on-year versus Electronics at 13.3%.
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.direction: A 24.2 percentage point growth gap with Other Engineering significantly outpacing Electronics expansion.
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Credit allocation favors heavy engineering and industrial machinery over electronics manufacturing, reflecting capital-intensive infrastructure buildout over consumer electronics.

sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.observation: Others commands 75.7% of food processing credit, followed by Sugar at 11.9%, Edible Oils at 10.4%, and Tea at 2.0%.
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.direction: Edible Oils strengthened while Others weakened, though Others maintains dominance with a 73.7 percentage point spread.
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.inference: Specialty food processing segments like edible oils are gaining credit allocation within the broader food sector, suggesting value-addition trends in agricultural processing.

sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Edible Oils & Vanaspati leads food processing growth at 23.9%, followed by Others at 14.8%, Sugar at 4.3%, and Tea at 0.8%.
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.direction: A 23.1 percentage point growth spread with Edible Oils significantly outperforming traditional segments like Sugar and Tea.
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Credit is shifting toward processed food manufacturing and oil refining over traditional commodities like sugar and tea, reflecting consumer demand for packaged food products.

sibc-industry-type-share-scan.observation: Infrastructure dominates industry credit with a 32.6% share, followed by Basic Metal and Metal Products at 11.3% and Other Industries at 7.6%.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.direction: The top 3 segments control over half of industry lending, while bottom segments like Leather (0.3%) and Glass (0.4%) remain minimal players.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.inference: Industry credit is heavily concentrated in infrastructure build-out and core manufacturing. Banks are backing India's capital formation story through power, transport, and metals financing.

sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.observation: Gems and Jewellery leads industry growth at 41.4%, followed by Petroleum/Coal at 32.5% and All Engineering at 32.2%.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.direction: Growth is broad-based with 7 active and 12 strengthening sectors, while laggards like Mining (7.2%) and Textiles (8.9%) still post positive growth.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.inference: The sector rotation is clear — banks are financing India's export push in gems, engineering goods, and energy transition. Even slow-growing traditional sectors remain in positive territory.

sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.observation: Power commands 56.6% of infrastructure credit, with Roads at 22.6% and Other Infrastructure at 12.0%.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.direction: The top 3 infrastructure categories control 91.2% of the segment, leaving transport modes like Airports (0.4%) and Railways (0.4%) as minimal shares.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.inference: Banks are heavily backing India's power sector expansion and road connectivity. The concentration suggests focused policy-driven lending rather than diversified infrastructure bets.

sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Ports surge 48.7% while core infrastructure segments show mixed performance — Power up 22.1%, Roads barely positive at 1.1%.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Transport infrastructure faces stress with Railways down 50.1%, Airports down 27.2%, and Telecoms down 12.2%.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Banks are financing India's export infrastructure (ports) aggressively while traditional transport sectors contract. This signals a shift toward trade-enabling infrastructure over domestic connectivity.

sibc-large-corporate-share.observation: Large corporates hold a 67.15% share of industry credit, up 0.02 points from the prior period.
sibc-large-corporate-share.direction: The share has stabilized around 67% after declining from 71.0% → 67.1% over the past year.
sibc-large-corporate-share.inference: The corporate credit market has found equilibrium. Large enterprises maintain dominant access to bank financing, but the recent decline suggests banks are gradually expanding to smaller players.

sibc-large-corporate-yoy.observation: Large corporate credit growth accelerated to 8.95%, the strongest reading in this 6-period window.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.direction: Growth momentum built from 7.8% → 8.9% over two periods, breaking above the historical range.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.inference: Banks are backing large corporate capex plans with renewed confidence. This acceleration signals corporate India's investment cycle is gaining momentum with institutional financing support.

sibc-msme-medium-share.observation: Medium enterprises hold a 9.65% share of industry credit, down marginally by 0.02 points.
sibc-msme-medium-share.direction: The share remains stable in the 9.7% range after climbing from 8.9% a year ago.
sibc-msme-medium-share.inference: Medium enterprises have secured a steady 1-in-10 share of industry financing. The stability suggests banks have found a sustainable allocation to this growth-critical segment.

sibc-msme-medium-yoy.observation: Medium enterprise credit growth reached 21.74%, accelerating 0.75 points and ranking in the upper range of recent performance.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.direction: Growth momentum has strengthened from 21.0% → 21.7% and consistently outpaces large corporates by 12+ percentage points.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.inference: Banks are aggressively financing the missing middle of Indian industry. This growth differential suggests policy-driven lending is successfully reaching scale-up businesses ahead of large enterprises.

sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.observation: Micro & Small enterprise credit posted 24.2pp full-year acceleration — the highest reading in this dataset.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.direction: Acceleration held at the maximum level of 24.2pp, unchanged from prior period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.inference: This signals entrenched momentum in small business lending, not a temporary spike. Banks are systematically expanding micro-enterprise exposure.

sibc-msme-micro-small-share.observation: Micro & Small enterprises claimed 23.20% of total industry credit — in the upper range of historical levels.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.direction: Share held steady at 23.2%, up from 20.0% → 23.5% over the past year.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.inference: Small businesses are winning a structurally larger slice of bank credit allocation. This reflects deliberate portfolio rebalancing toward smaller ticket lending.

sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.observation: Micro & Small enterprise credit surged 33.14% year-on-year — the fastest growth rate on record.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 30.39% → 33.14%, extending the climb from single digits a year ago.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.inference: Banks are aggressively expanding small business books after years of tepid growth. This pace signals a fundamental shift in credit allocation strategy.

sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.observation: MSME credit outpaced large corporate lending by 24.2 percentage points — in the upper range of this series.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.direction: The growth gap widened from 22.6pp → 24.2pp, up from just 2.8pp a year ago.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.inference: Banks are clearly favoring smaller borrowers over large corporates. This divergence suggests either risk concerns about large deals or deliberate small-ticket strategy.

sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.observation: Other Textiles dominates at 44.6% of textile credit, followed by Cotton Textiles at 36.5%.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.direction: All four textile sub-segments remain active with a 43.0% spread between largest and smallest.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.inference: Textile lending is concentrated in non-cotton segments, likely reflecting synthetic fiber and technical textile expansion over traditional cotton manufacturing.

sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.observation: All four textile segments posted positive growth, led by Jute Textiles at 16.5% and Other Textiles at 15.9%.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Broad-based acceleration with Other Textiles and Cotton Textiles both upgrading to strengthening status.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Even traditional manufacturing sectors like textiles are benefiting from the credit expansion. The universal positive growth signals broad industrial optimism, not just tech or services.

retail.narrative: Personal loans are experiencing a secured credit boom led by an explosive 123% surge in gold loans and robust 18.6% growth in vehicle financing. Housing and Other Personal Loans are growing solidly at 13%, while unsecured segments like consumer durables are contracting 5.3% and credit card outstanding manages only 3.5% growth. Banks are clearly pivoting toward collateral-backed personal lending, with gold loans now driving disproportionate growth despite representing a smaller share of the overall market.

sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.observation: Credit card growth has stayed below 10% for 3 consecutive periods — the longest such streak on record.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.direction: Extended from 2 periods to 3 periods, reaching the maximum streak length in this dataset.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.inference: Credit card demand has entered a sustained weak phase, suggesting consumer spending restraint is deepening rather than recovering.

sibc-pl-cc-share.observation: Credit cards now represent 4.24% of personal loans, down from 4.30% in the prior period.
sibc-pl-cc-share.direction: Share declined 5.0% → 4.2% over the past year, hitting the lowest level in 6 periods.
sibc-pl-cc-share.inference: Credit cards are losing ground within personal loans as weak growth causes market share erosion to faster-growing segments.

sibc-pl-cc-yoy.observation: Credit card outstanding grew 3.55% year-on-year in April 2026, up from 1.75% in March.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.direction: Growth collapsed 13.0% → 1.5% from February 2025 to February 2026, then recovered slightly to 3.5%.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.inference: The credit card sector shows early signs of bottoming out but remains in single-digit growth territory — a dramatic slowdown from double-digit norms.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.observation: Consumer durables lending has contracted for 4 consecutive periods, the longest negative streak in this window.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.direction: Negative streak extended from 3 periods to 4 periods, approaching the historical maximum of 5 periods.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.inference: Consumer durables financing is in sustained contraction, reflecting weak demand for cars, appliances, and discretionary purchases.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.observation: Consumer durables represent just 0.32% of personal loans, unchanged from the prior period.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.direction: Share dropped 0.4% → 0.3% over the past year, reaching the lowest level in this dataset.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.inference: Consumer durables have become a marginal segment within personal lending as the category remains in persistent decline.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.observation: Consumer durables lending contracted 5.34% year-on-year, an improvement from the 9.78% decline in March.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.direction: Contraction eased from -9.8% to -5.3%, but remains negative for the fourth straight period.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.inference: While the pace of decline is moderating, consumer durables financing continues to shrink as discretionary spending remains weak.

sibc-pl-education-share.observation: Education loans account for 2.25% of personal loans, down slightly from 2.29% in the prior period.
sibc-pl-education-share.direction: Share has edged down 2.3% → 2.2% over the past year, reaching the minimum in this window.
sibc-pl-education-share.inference: Education loans maintain a stable but small share of personal lending, with slight erosion as other segments recover.

sibc-pl-education-yoy.observation: Education loans grew 13.38% year-on-year, down from 13.72% in the prior period.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.direction: Growth has gradually decelerated 15.9% → 13.4% over the past year but remains in double digits.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.inference: Education loans are the standout performer in personal lending, delivering consistent double-digit growth as families prioritize skill investment despite economic uncertainty.

sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.observation: Advances against Fixed Deposits reached 2.44% of personal loans, the highest level in this 6-period window.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.direction: Share climbed from 2.27% to 2.44%, adding 0.17 percentage points.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.inference: This niche secured lending product is gaining ground as banks seek lower-risk exposure in personal loan portfolios.

sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.observation: FD-backed advances surged 19.22% year-on-year, accelerating sharply from 14.07% growth last period.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.direction: Growth trajectory jumped 14.07% → 19.22%, the fastest pace in this dataset.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.inference: Banks are aggressively expanding collateralised personal lending as a safer alternative to unsecured products.

sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.observation: Gold loans have grown every period for 6 consecutive months, the longest unbroken run on record.
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.direction: Positive streak extended from 5 to 6 periods, marking a new maximum.
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.inference: Gold lending has become the most reliable growth driver in personal loans, with no signs of momentum breaking.

sibc-pl-gold-share.observation: Gold loans captured 6.63% of all personal lending, near the peak of this 6-period range.
sibc-pl-gold-share.direction: Share rose 6.31% → 6.63%, continuing a dramatic climb from 3.0% a year ago.
sibc-pl-gold-share.inference: Gold loans have doubled their portfolio weight in 12 months, reshaping the personal lending mix toward secured products.

sibc-pl-gold-yoy.observation: Gold loan growth held at 123.15%, slightly cooling from the 127.90% peak but remaining above 120%.
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.direction: Growth eased 127.90% → 123.15%, still more than doubling year-on-year volumes.
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.inference: Even with marginal deceleration, gold lending continues expanding at unprecedented rates as borrowers pledge jewelry for credit access.

sibc-pl-housing-share.observation: Housing loans fell to 48.35% of personal lending, the smallest share in this 6-period window.
sibc-pl-housing-share.direction: Portfolio weight dropped 48.64% → 48.35%, extending a slide from over 50% a year ago.
sibc-pl-housing-share.inference: Housing's traditional dominance is eroding as faster-growing secured products like gold capture market share.

sibc-pl-housing-yoy.observation: Housing loan growth strengthened to 11.48%, the best reading in this dataset.
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated 10.98% → 11.48%, reaching the upper end of its recent range.
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.inference: Despite losing share, housing credit is expanding at a healthy pace, suggesting robust underlying mortgage demand.

sibc-pl-other-share.observation: Other personal loans held 24.99% of the portfolio, near the bottom of this period's range.
sibc-pl-other-share.direction: Share edged down 25.05% → 24.99%, continuing a gradual decline from 25.8% levels.
sibc-pl-other-share.inference: Miscellaneous unsecured lending is ceding ground to specialized secured products as risk appetite tightens.

sibc-pl-other-yoy.observation: Other Personal Loans grew 12.98% year-on-year, the strongest reading in this 6-period window.
sibc-pl-other-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 12.09% → 12.98%, building on a steady climb from 8.2% in February 2025.
sibc-pl-other-yoy.inference: Non-housing, non-vehicle personal credit is gaining momentum as banks expand into general consumer financing beyond traditional secured categories.

sibc-pl-share-advances-share.observation: Advances against shares and bonds held steady at 0.15% of total personal loans.
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.direction: Share remained flat at 0.15% from prior period, fluctuating within a narrow 0.1-0.2% band.
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.inference: Securities-backed lending remains a niche product with minimal market penetration despite recent growth pickup.

sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.observation: Advances against shares and bonds grew 4.63% year-on-year, the fastest pace in three periods.
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.direction: Growth picked up from 2.82% → 4.63%, recovering from the sharp deceleration off 33% highs in early 2025.
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.inference: Securities-backed lending is stabilising after the volatile swing from boom to near-stagnation, suggesting a more sustainable growth trajectory.

sibc-pl-share-scan.observation: Housing dominates personal loans at 48.3% share, followed by Other Personal Loans at 25.0% and Vehicle Loans at 10.6%.
sibc-pl-share-scan.direction: The top three categories control 84% of the market, with niche segments like advances against shares (0.2%) and consumer durables (0.3%) representing minimal share.
sibc-pl-share-scan.inference: Personal credit remains concentrated in housing and general-purpose loans, with secured vehicle financing the only other material segment — suggesting limited product diversification across banks.

sibc-pl-vehicle-share.observation: Vehicle loans held 10.64% of personal loans, essentially unchanged from the prior period.
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.direction: Share slipped marginally from 10.68% → 10.64%, staying within the tight 10.4-10.7% range of recent periods.
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.inference: Vehicle lending share is stable despite strong growth, indicating the broader personal loans pool is expanding at a similar pace.

sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.observation: Vehicle loans grew 18.61% year-on-year, the fastest pace in this 6-period window.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 17.14% → 18.61%, more than doubling from the 8.6% low in April 2025.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.inference: Auto financing is in a pronounced upturn as consumer demand for vehicles rebounds and banks compete aggressively in this secured lending segment.

sibc-pl-yoy-scan.observation: Gold loans exploded 123.1% year-on-year, dwarfing all other personal loan categories, while Consumer Durables contracted 5.3%.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.direction: The growth spread widened to 128.5 percentage points between gold loans and consumer durables, with Vehicle Loans (18.6%) and Fixed Deposit advances (19.2%) also showing strong momentum.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.inference: Personal credit is polarising sharply — secured lending against gold and vehicles is booming while unsecured consumer financing stagnates, reflecting banks' preference for collateral-backed exposure amid economic uncertainty.

psl.narrative: Priority sector lending shows a tale of two markets: housing rockets ahead at nearly 40% growth alongside strong micro enterprise expansion, while trade-linked categories like export credit contract sharply. Renewable energy maintains its 6-period growth streak despite cooling from explosive levels, and social infrastructure has bounced back from contraction. Banks are clearly prioritizing domestic-focused PSL categories over export-dependent ones, with housing emerging as the standout winner in the post-pandemic credit cycle.

sibc-psl-agri-yoy.observation: PSL agriculture credit grew 16.03% year-on-year, reaching its strongest pace in this 6-period window.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 15.20% → 16.03%, building on a steady climb from 11.0% in April 2025.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.inference: Rural credit demand is gaining momentum as agricultural income improves, supporting both seasonal working capital and farm mechanisation lending.

sibc-psl-education-yoy.observation: PSL educational loans expanded 23.02% year-on-year, maintaining near-peak growth despite a slight cooling.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.direction: Growth eased from 23.96% → 23.02% after surging from near-zero levels a year ago.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.inference: Student loan demand remains robust as higher education costs rise, but banks may be tightening underwriting after the rapid expansion.

sibc-psl-export-yoy.observation: PSL export credit contracted 8.36% year-on-year, though the decline is moderating from deeper losses.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.direction: Contraction narrowed from -14.09% → -8.36%, recovering from a low of -17.2% in February 2026.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.inference: Export financing remains under pressure from global trade headwinds, but the improving trend suggests banks expect export recovery in coming quarters.

sibc-psl-housing-yoy.observation: PSL housing credit surged 39.80% year-on-year, hitting the highest growth rate in this window.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth strengthened from 38.25% → 39.80%, a dramatic turnaround from negative territory a year ago.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.inference: Affordable housing demand is booming as interest rates stabilise and government incentives drive first-time buyer activity.

sibc-psl-medium-yoy.observation: PSL medium enterprise credit grew 17.85% year-on-year, marking the slowest pace in this 6-period window.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 18.39% → 17.85%, down from peaks above 22% in early 2025.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.inference: Mid-market businesses are facing tighter credit conditions as banks shift focus to retail and micro-enterprise lending with better risk profiles.

sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.observation: PSL MSME credit acceleration held at 16.1 percentage points on a full-year basis, maintaining maximum acceleration.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.direction: The acceleration rate remained unchanged at 16.1 points across all periods, showing sustained momentum.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.inference: MSME lending has structurally shifted to a higher growth trajectory, driven by government guarantee schemes and digital lending platforms reducing friction.

sibc-psl-msme-yoy.observation: PSL micro and small enterprise credit expanded 29.53% year-on-year, reaching the strongest growth in this window.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 26.49% → 29.53%, more than doubling from 13.0% levels a year ago.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.inference: Small business credit is experiencing a lending boom as digital platforms scale and government guarantee coverage reduces bank risk aversion.

sibc-psl-others-yoy.observation: PSL other categories contracted 9.55% year-on-year, though the decline is the smallest in this window.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.direction: Contraction narrowed sharply from -19.41% → -9.55%, recovering from a trough of -23.9% in February 2026.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.inference: Specialty PSL categories are stabilising as banks complete portfolio rebalancing and focus on core agriculture, MSME, and housing segments.

sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.observation: Renewable energy lending has grown for 6 consecutive periods, the longest positive streak on record.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.direction: Extended from 5 to 6 periods, reaching the historical maximum.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.inference: Banks are sustaining their renewable energy push despite recent growth moderation, signaling structural commitment to green financing mandates.

sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.observation: Renewable energy lending grew 34.13%, down from 64.36% in the prior period.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated by 30.24 points, falling to the weakest reading in 6 periods.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.inference: The green lending boom is cooling after explosive growth phases, but double-digit expansion suggests the sector remains structurally attractive.

sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.observation: Social infrastructure lending grew 12.88%, reversing from -1.02% contraction in the prior period.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.direction: Swung positive by 13.90 points, moving from decline to expansion.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.inference: Banks are re-engaging with social infrastructure after a brief pullback, likely driven by government spending on healthcare and education facilities.

sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.observation: Weaker sections lending grew 11.07%, down from 13.55% in the prior period.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.direction: Growth slowed by 2.47 points, reaching the weakest level in 6 periods.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.inference: Financial inclusion momentum is softening as banks prioritize higher-yield PSL categories over mandated weaker section targets.

sibc-psl-yoy-scan.observation: Housing leads PSL growth at 39.8%, while export credit contracts 8.4% and others decline 9.6%.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.direction: Spread of 49.4% between top and bottom performers, with 4 categories strengthening status.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.inference: PSL deployment is bifurcating sharply — housing and micro enterprises drive growth while trade-linked sectors struggle. Renewable energy and social infrastructure show renewed banker confidence.
